opp.name: ASYAD_ S2P_2026
opp.next_step: Confidence 10% June 8th - Follow up demo scheduled Thurs 11th. June 4th - Positive, but short discovery call held, early stage exploration, will require an RFP in due course (no compelling event, likely Q4) next step is to arrange another session to capture more info on the project, provide a conceptual overview for the stakeholders then run a live demo, date TBC w/c 8th Jun. SITUATION ASYAD Group runs fragmented procurement across multiple business units — using a government e-tendering platform, SAP Ariba (for RFQs/tenders), and a partially-deployed SAP S/4HANA ERP (in progress). Each BU operates with different systems and methodologies. IMPACT/PAIN No unified spend visibility; data is siloed across BUs; manual/inconsistent processes; limited strategic supplier depth; slow access to contract information; tail spend poorly managed. PROPOSED RESOLUTION/STRATEGY A flexible, integrated S2P platform that stitches all systems together (including SAP S/4HANA and the government e-tendering tool), provide a control-tower view of spend/data, and is highly configurable.
opp.ais_why: ASYAD is actively pursuing digital transformation as part of Oman's logistics and economic diversification strategy. As a large integrated logistics and transportation group, procurement plays a critical role in managing complex supplier ecosystems and operational spend. The organization is likely to benefit from AI-powered procurement capabilities such as autonomous sourcing, intake management, contract lifecycle management, supplier risk monitoring, spend analytics, and procurement automation. Its focus on operational efficiency, data-driven decision-making, and innovation makes ASYAD a strong candidate for Agentic AI-powered Source-to-Pay (S2P) solutions.

metrics.narrative: Business problem clearly articulated but not yet quantified. Jamila stated on 4 Jun 2026 call: fragmented procurement across BUs with no unified spend visibility, data siloed, manual processes, limited strategic supplier depth (tenders re-floated multiple times), slow contract access (takes 1-2 days across BUs), and tail spend poorly managed. No ROI, cost baseline, cycle-time reduction, or headcount savings quantified. MEDDPICC CRM notes 'no ROI/TCO framework exists to justify $300k investment to CFO/EB'. The qualitative pain is solid; the quantified business case is a gap and must be built to justify procurement approval.

champion.narrative: Jamila Al Bukhaly, Senior Manager Contracts & Procurement Shared Services, is the operational champion. MEDDPICC 2.0 states 'She attended Gartner, was impressed by Zycus, briefed Sameh, initiated contact, owns the RFP process, and is the operational driver of this evaluation.' She led the 4 Jun discovery call, requested the 11 Jun demo, and provided detailed pain/criteria articulation. Evidence of advocacy: she initiated the engagement after Gartner, brought Sameh (her SVP) to the demo, and is driving the RFP timeline. Access to EB: direct report to the relevant stakeholder engagement: active as of 11 Jun demo (20 days ago), but no follow-up action recorded since then. Champion strength is developing—she has access and is operationally driving, but has not yet demonstrated ability to navigate internal politics (SAP consolidation pressure) or secure executive-level buy-in beyond Sameh's passive attendance.

competition.narrative: SAP Ariba is the incumbent RFQ/tender tool and the primary competitive threat. Buyer is mid-SAP S/4HANA ERP rollout, creating internal momentum toward SAP ecosystem consolidation. MEDDPICC 2.0 states 'There is likely internal political momentum toward SAP ecosystem consolidation (one vendor, one support contract, one integration) even if Ariba is not functionally superior.' Jamila indicated on 4 Jun call that Ariba is under review for replacement due to supplier charges (friction) and usability. Zycus differentiators articulated in MEDDPICC 2.0: no supplier charges, modern UI, native module integration, pre-built SAP connectors, self-service configurability, Merlin AI. Competitors__c also lists Coupa, GEP Worldwide, and iValua, but none mentioned on calls. Wedge: position Zycus as best-of-breed with superior SAP integration vs Ariba's rigidity, and make Merlin AI the Phase 2 differentiator for autonomous sourcing. Risk: if SAP program lead or CFO has strategic preference for SAP consolidation, Jamila's operational case may be overruled.

sources: Avoma discovery call 4 Jun 2026 (Jamila Al Bukhaly)
identify_pain.narrative: Operational and strategic pain clearly articulated by Jamila on 4 Jun 2026 call. Specific pains: (1) Fragmented systems—no single platform across BUs; government e-tendering, SAP Ariba RFQ tool, and partially-deployed SAP S/4HANA ERP all disconnected. (2) No unified spend/data visibility—'control tower' reporting impossible. (3) Limited strategic supplier depth—'sometimes tenders must be re-floated multiple times to find competent suppliers' (Avoma note). (4) Slow contract access—'locating specific contracts across business units can take a day or two' (Avoma). (5) Tail spend poorly managed—no robust automated tool. (6) Manual/inconsistent sourcing processes limiting automation. (7) Governance adaptability—current tools too rigid for dynamic process changes; 'need a flexible, configurable system that adapts to changing governance without lengthy vendor dependency' (Avoma). Pain owner: Jamila (operational) and Sameh (strategic). Pain is confirmed and specific; quantification is the gap.

paper_process.narrative: Contracting mechanics not yet discussed (no MSA, SOW, legal review, or InfoSec mentioned on calls). Government holding company likely requires legal review by government counsel and Board-level approval depending on contract value. MEDDPICC 2.0 flags 'whether the procurement process requires public tender, lowest-bid preference, or government authority sign-off' as unknown. Integration scoping for SAP S/4HANA and Oman government e-tendering platform will be part of SOW but feasibility not yet validated. No SI/partner role mentioned. Paper process clarity is a gap; need to surface approval chain, legal review requirements, and whether public tender rules apply during the business-case / pre-RFP phase.

sources: Avoma touchpoint manifest 11 Jun 2026 (Sameh attended demo)
economic_buyer.narrative: Sameh Bartok, SVP Contracts & Procurement, is the economic buyer. MEDDPICC 2.0 states 'He attended the call, confirmed strategic direction, and holds executive authority. Any system procurement requires his sanction.' He attended the 11 Jun demo (touchpoint manifest). Budget is currently assigned to the SAP S/4HANA ERP rollout; S2P is under review within that envelope. Sameh is the likely budget authority but has not yet explicitly confirmed $300k spend approval. His engagement is passive (attended one demo, no active sponsorship signals). In a $300k group-level platform decision at a government holding company, this level of EB engagement is appropriate for early evaluation, but active sign-off and ROI validation are still required before RFP sanction.

decision_process.narrative: Formal tender/RFP process required per ASYAD procurement governance. MEDDPICC 2.0 states 'The RFP will be owned and managed by Jamila's team. Decisions made by Jamila and Sameh in concert with stakeholders (steering group likely involved). Process sequence: exploration → demo → internal business case → formal RFP → sanction authority approval.' Buyer stated on 4 Jun call 'will require an RFP in due course, no compelling event, likely Q4'. Current stage: exploration and demo complete (11 Jun), awaiting internal business case build and RFP drafting. Unknown: whether public tender rules, lowest-bid preference, or government oversight apply (government holding company), and whether Board or ministerial sign-off is required for $300k contracts. These procurement rules must be clarified to forecast cycle time accurately.

sources: Avoma discovery call 4 Jun 2026 (Jamila Al Bukhaly)
decision_criteria.narrative: Decision criteria explicitly stated by Jamila on 4 Jun 2026 call and captured in MEDDPICC 2.0: (1) Integration with SAP S/4HANA, government e-tendering platform, and RFQ tool; (2) Self-service configurability—team must add fields/forms without vendor dependency; (3) Data hosting in-country (Oman governance requirement); (4) Unified spend/data visibility ('control tower' view); (5) Flexibility—adapts to dynamic governance; (6) Supplier management depth; (7) Cost efficiency vs Ariba. Avoma notes confirm 'Configuration and flexibility are required: solution must be highly configurable or allow apps on top so Asyad can adapt forms/fields quickly without heavy code changes' and 'Data hosting requirement: governance requires data to be hosted in Oman'. These are the RFP scoring criteria; Oman hosting is a hard knockout requirement.

items.detail: Oman data hosting is a hard governance constraint stated by Jamila on 4 Jun 2026 but not validated by Zycus infrastructure/legal teams or addressed in the 11 Jun demo. If Zycus cannot host in-region and a competitor (e.g., SAP Ariba via SAP regional data centers) can confirm Oman hosting, the deal is lost on a technical knockout regardless of functional fit.

Single-thread to operational Procurement Manager (Jamila) with no CFO, CIO, or C-level engagement. In a $300k group-level platform decision at a government holding company, budget authority and strategic alignment sit above Jamila's level. If executives have not bought into the S2P initiative or prefer a different vendor, Jamila's advocacy alone cannot overcome that. Competitors with exec access will outmaneuver Zycus.

Buyer stated 'no compelling event, likely Q4 RFP timing' on 4 Jun 2026. Without urgency or a deadline forcing the decision (e.g., contract expiration, regulatory mandate, Board directive), the project could drift, deprioritize, or stall indefinitely. The 273-day runway is appropriate for government tender cycle but also means competitors have months to build relationships, shape the RFP, and multi-thread. If Zycus does not maintain continuous engagement and executive access, the deal will go cold or be lost to a more aggressive competitor.

SAP Ariba is the incumbent and ASYAD is mid-flight on SAP S/4HANA ERP implementation. There is likely internal political momentum toward SAP ecosystem consolidation ('one vendor, one support contract, one integration') even if Ariba is not functionally superior. If the ERP program lead or CFO has a strategic preference for SAP, the operational case for Zycus may be overruled. Zycus must map the SAP S/4HANA program lead and CFO urgently to understand the consolidation vs best-of-breed appetite.

Government holding company procurement may involve the relevant stakeholder oversight, legal review by government counsel, and Board-level approval depending on contract value. None of this has been surfaced by the relevant stakeholder the procurement process requires public tender, lowest-bid preference, or government authority sign-off, the sales cycle could extend significantly beyond 31 Mar 2027, and Zycus's ability to compete on value (vs price alone) may be constrained. Need to clarify procurement rules urgently.

Integration with Oman government e-tendering platform is required (buyer stated 'stitches all systems together including the government e-tendering tool') but feasibility is unknown. Need to validate whether Zycus has integrated with this platform before or can scope custom integration, and surface in next demo.

north_star_verdict.headline: Early-stage deal progressing on pace: discovery held 4 Jun, live demo delivered 11 Jun (20 days ago), RFP planned for Q4 2026. At 273 days to close (31 Mar 2027), the runway is appropriate for a government-holding-company formal tender cycle. Key risks are single-thread (only Jamila engaged; EB Sameh attended one call but not yet actively sponsoring), Oman data-hosting constraint unresolved, and SAP ecosystem consolidation pressure.

flags: SHAPE THE RFP (Q4 2026 drop): With 3-4 months before RFP release, Zycus has a narrow but critical window to influence the evaluation criteria in our favor. The buyer has not yet formalized requirements; Jamila stated 'will require an RFP in due course' but no draft exists. If Zycus waits passively, Ariba (the incumbent) or a competitor with early exec access will write the RFP around their strengths. Immediate action: offer to co-author a requirements matrix or evaluation scorecard with Jamila, positioning it as 'helping ASYAD ensure the RFP captures all the integration, configurability, and AI capabilities you need.' Tilt the scorecard toward Zycus differentiators: (1) self-service configurability (no vendor dependency for form/field changes), (2) integration depth scored on SAP S/4 + government e-tendering + legacy systems (3-way, not 1-way), (3) Merlin AI for tail-spend automation and supplier intelligence as a scored criterion (none of the competitors match this), (4) Oman data hosting compliance as a pass/fail gate (may eliminate Coupa or others). Work with Jamila to embed these as weighted criteria NOW, before the RFP committee formalizes the document. This is how Zycus wins before the official RFP evaluation even begins.

No compelling event: buyer stated 'no compelling event, likely Q4' RFP timing (Next Step 4 Jun 2026). With 9+ months to close and no urgency, deal is at high risk of drift, deprioritization, or being shaped by competitors.

Champion strength: developing, not strong. Jamila is engaged and articulated pain, but has not demonstrated EB access, advocacy against SAP Ariba, or executive alignment. Assess her champion strength by asking for intro to budget owner and SAP program lead; if she cannot deliver, she is a coach, not a champion.
best_practice_check.summary: Early-stage deal with strong operational champion (Jamila) but single-threaded and missing C-level engagement. EB Sameh attended demo but is passively engaged. SAP consolidation pressure is the primary competitive risk. Three highest-leverage actions: (1) map and engage CFO + CIO + SAP program lead to surface consolidation appetite and build executive sponsorship, (2) validate Oman data hosting feasibility urgently (hard knockout requirement), (3) co-author quantified ROI/TCO business case with Jamila to justify $300k spend and arm her for internal advocacy.

competitive_position.summary: SAP Ariba is the strongest current threat as the incumbent RFQ/tender tool and beneficiary of SAP S/4HANA ERP consolidation momentum. Buyer is mid-ERP rollout, creating internal political pressure toward 'one vendor, one support, one integration' even if Ariba is functionally inferior. Jamila stated on 4 Jun that Ariba is under review for replacement due to supplier charges and usability, but if the SAP program lead or CFO prefers ecosystem consolidation, her operational case may be overruled. Coupa, GEP, and iValua are listed in Competitors__c but not mentioned on any call or activity; threat level unknown. Wedge: Zycus offers superior SAP integration (pre-built connectors, faster/cheaper than Ariba), no supplier charges (vs Ariba friction), modern UI driving adoption, and Merlin AI for autonomous sourcing—position as best-of-breed that integrates better with SAP than Ariba does.

hard.ais_why: ASYAD is actively pursuing digital transformation as part of Oman's logistics and economic diversification strategy. As a large integrated logistics and transportation group, procurement plays a critical role in managing complex supplier ecosystems and operational spend. The organization is likely to benefit from AI-powered procurement capabilities such as autonomous sourcing, intake management, contract lifecycle management, supplier risk monitoring, spend analytics, and procurement automation. Its focus on operational efficiency, data-driven decision-making, and innovation makes ASYAD a strong candidate for Agentic AI-powered Source-to-Pay (S2P) solutions.
hard.next_step:
Confidence 10%

June 8th - Follow up demo scheduled Thurs 11th.

June 4th - Positive, but short discovery call held, early stage exploration, will require an RFP in due course (no compelling event, likely Q4) next step is to arrange another session to capture more info on the project, provide a conceptual overview for the stakeholders then run a live demo, date TBC w/c 8th Jun.

SITUATION

ASYAD Group runs fragmented procurement across multiple business units — using a government e-tendering platform, SAP Ariba (for RFQs/tenders), and a partially-deployed SAP S/4HANA ERP (in progress). Each BU operates with different systems and methodologies. 

IMPACT/PAIN

No unified spend visibility; data is siloed across BUs; manual/inconsistent processes; limited strategic supplier depth; slow access to contract information; tail spend poorly managed. 

PROPOSED RESOLUTION/STRATEGY

A flexible, integrated S2P platform that stitches all systems together (including SAP S/4HANA and the government e-tendering tool), provide a control-tower view of spend/data, and is highly configurable.

deltas.subject: Cooling engagement: last verified buyer activity 11 Jun (demo date), 20 days silent as of 01 Jul. Rep outreach 08 Jun but no buyer reply or next-step confirmation. Risk of ghost if not re-engaged within 7 days.

Oman data hosting is a hard governance constraint stated by Jamila (4 Jun 2026) but not validated by Zycus infrastructure/legal teams or addressed in the demo. If Zycus cannot meet this requirement, the deal is dead regardless of functional fit. Competitors may also struggle with this, but if one (e.g., SAP Ariba via SAP's regional infrastructure) can confirm Oman hosting and Zycus cannot, we lose on a technical knockout.

Buyer stated 'no compelling event, likely Q4' RFP timing on 4 Jun 2026, and close date is set to 31 Mar 2027 (278 days out, 9+ months). Without urgency or a deadline forcing the decision (e.g., contract expiration, regulatory mandate, Board directive), the project could drift, deprioritize, or stall indefinitely. The long runway is appropriate for enterprise procurement, but it also means competitors have months to build relationships, shape the RFP, and multi-thread. If Zycus does not maintain continuous engagement and executive access, the deal will go cold or be lost to a more aggressive competitor.

SAP Ariba is the incumbent and ASYAD is mid-flight on SAP S/4HANA ERP implementation. There is likely internal political momentum toward SAP ecosystem consolidation ('one vendor, one support contract, one integration') even if Ariba is not functionally superior. If the ERP program lead or CFO has a strategic preference for SAP, Jamila's operational case for Zycus may be overruled. Zycus must map the SAP S/4HANA program lead and CFO urgently to understand the consolidation vs best-of-breed appetite.

Integration with Oman government e-tendering platform is required (buyer stated 'stitches all systems together including the government e-tendering tool') but feasibility is unknown. Need to validate whether Zycus has integrated with this platform before or can scope custom integration, and surface in next demo.

No compelling event: buyer stated 'no compelling event, likely Q4' RFP timing (4 Jun 2026). With 277 days to close and no urgency, project could drift or deprioritize. Need to surface or create a forcing function (e.g., SAP S/4HANA ERP go-live dependency, contract expiration, Board directive) to accelerate decision timeline.

Budget is currently assigned to the active ERP rollout project (SAP S/4HANA). The S2P component is under review for optimisation within that envelope. Sameh is the likely budget authority, but this was not explicitly confirmed per MEDDPICC CRM Budget Owner entry (8 Jun 2026). The $300k S2P investment case has not been validated as approved, and no ROI/TCO framework exists to justify it. If the ERP budget is exhausted or the S2P business case is not built, the deal may stall or be deferred beyond 31 Mar 2027.

Government holding company procurement may involve relevant stakeholder oversight, legal review by government counsel, and Board-level approval depending on contract value. MEDDPICC CRM Blockers (8 Jun 2026) raises whether 'the procurement process requires public tender, lowest-bid preference, or government authority sign-off'. None of this has been surfaced by Jamila or Sameh. If the RFP must follow public tender rules or lowest-bid preference, Zycus's ability to compete on value (vs price alone) may be constrained, and the sales cycle could extend significantly beyond 31 Mar 2027.

Single-thread to operational Procurement Manager with no EB, CFO, CIO, or C-level engagement. Competitors with exec access will outmaneuver Zycus.

No champion: Jamila is cooperative but not advocating; no evidence of EB access, internal advocacy, or deal-driving behavior. Cooling signal: no engagement in 20 days post-scheduled demo.

Incumbent risk not addressed: SAP Ariba is the incumbent and ASYAD is mid-SAP S/4HANA ERP rollout, creating ecosystem consolidation momentum. Zycus has not yet positioned the best-of-breed integration wedge or validated ability to win against SAP's political advantage.

Hard constraint not validated: Oman data hosting is a hard governance requirement (Jamila, 4 Jun 2026) but Zycus has not confirmed feasibility with infrastructure/legal teams or provided written confirmation. If Zycus cannot meet this, deal is dead.
No quantified value case: operational pain is clear but no ROI/TCO business case built to justify $300k investment to CFO/EB when budget is assigned to ERP rollout.
Post-demo black hole: demo scheduled 11 Jun (Next Step 8 Jun), but no evidence it occurred, no feedback, no next step, no buyer engagement in 20 days since.

MULTI-THREAD NOW: Single-thread to Jamila (operational Procurement Manager, no budget authority) is the top win-probability risk in a $300k, 9-month enterprise deal requiring group-level EB/CFO approval and cross-BU coordination. In a government holding company, the operational lead rarely controls the commercial decision. Competitors (Ariba, GEP, iValua, Coupa) will multi-thread to IT, finance, and executives; Zycus must do so first. Immediate action: ask Jamila to map internal stakeholders ('Who else should be part of this conversation?') and request introductions to (a) the group CFO or budget holder for the S2P investment, (b) the CIO or SAP S/4HANA program lead (integration is a stated top priority), (c) at least one BU procurement lead to validate cross-BU pain and buy-in. Add all introduced contacts as OpportunityContactRoles within 14 days. Multi-threading is the single highest-leverage action to raise win probability before the Q4 RFP.

SHAPE THE RFP (Q4 2026 drop): With 3-4 months before RFP release, Zycus has a narrow but critical window to influence the evaluation criteria in our favor. The buyer has not yet formalized requirements; Jamila stated 'will require an RFP in due course' but no draft exists. If Zycus waits passively, Ariba (the incumbent) or a competitor with early exec access will write the RFP around their strengths. Immediate action: offer to co-author a requirements matrix or evaluation scorecard with Jamila, positioning it as 'helping ASYAD ensure the RFP captures all the integration, configurability, and AI capabilities you need.' Tilt the scorecard toward Zycus differentiators: (1) self-service configurability (no vendor dependency for form/field changes), (2) integration depth scored on SAP S/4 + government e-tendering + legacy systems (3-way, not 1-way), (3) Merlin AI for tail-spend automation and supplier intelligence as a scored criterion (none of the competitors match this), (4) Oman data hosting compliance as a pass/fail gate (may eliminate Coupa or others). Work with Jamila to embed these as weighted criteria NOW, before the RFP committee formalizes the document. This is how Zycus wins before the official RFP evaluation even begins.

OMAN HOSTING COMPLIANCE — VALIDATE IN WRITING BY 3 JUL 2026: Jamila stated 'governance requires data to be hosted in Oman (local hosting constraint)' on 4 Jun 2026. value.flag: MULTI-THREAD NOW: Single-thread to Jamila (operational Procurement Manager, no budget authority) is the top win-probability risk in a $300k, 9-month enterprise deal requiring group-level EB/CFO approval and cross-BU coordination. In a government holding company, the operational lead rarely controls the commercial decision. Competitors (Ariba, GEP, iValua, Coupa) will multi-thread to IT, finance, and executives; Zycus must do so first. Immediate action: ask Jamila to map internal stakeholders ('Who else should be part of this conversation?') and request introductions to (a) the group CFO or budget holder for the S2P investment, (b) the CIO or SAP S/4HANA program lead (integration is a stated top priority), (c) at least one BU procurement lead to validate cross-BU pain and buy-in. Add all introduced contacts as OpportunityContactRoles within 14 days. Multi-threading is the single highest-leverage action to raise win probability before the Q4 RFP.

value.flag: SHAPE THE RFP (Q4 2026 drop): With 3-4 months before RFP release, Zycus has a narrow but critical window to influence the evaluation criteria in our favor. The buyer has not yet formalized requirements; Jamila stated 'will require an RFP in due course' but no draft exists. If Zycus waits passively, Ariba (the incumbent) or a competitor with early exec access will write the RFP around their strengths. Immediate action: offer to co-author a requirements matrix or evaluation scorecard with Jamila, positioning it as 'helping ASYAD ensure the RFP captures all the integration, configurability, and AI capabilities you need.' Tilt the scorecard toward Zycus differentiators: (1) self-service configurability (no vendor dependency for form/field changes), (2) integration depth scored on SAP S/4 + government e-tendering + legacy systems (3-way, not 1-way), (3) Merlin AI for tail-spend automation and supplier intelligence as a scored criterion (none of the competitors match this), (4) Oman data hosting compliance as a pass/fail gate (may eliminate Coupa or others). Work with Jamila to embed these as weighted criteria NOW, before the RFP committee formalizes the document. This is how Zycus wins before the official RFP evaluation even begins.

value.flag: OMAN HOSTING COMPLIANCE — VALIDATE IN WRITING BY 3 JUL 2026: Jamila stated 'governance requires data to be hosted in Oman (local hosting constraint)' on 4 Jun 2026. This is a HARD, non-negotiable requirement that could disqualify Zycus if not met, regardless of functional superiority. It was NOT addressed in the 11 Jun demo or any follow-up, creating uncontrolled execution risk. Immediate action: (1) escalate to Zycus infrastructure/legal teams by 28 Jun to confirm whether Zycus can host data in Oman (on-prem, Oman-based cloud partner, or contractual data residency guarantee), (2) if yes, document the solution in a written compliance statement and send to Jamila by 3 Jul 2026 (within 7 days), citing specific hosting location, data sovereignty controls, and any certifications, (3) if no, escalate to Product/Exec leadership immediately as a deal-blocking issue requiring a workaround or disqualification decision. ALSO use this as a competitive wedge: ask Jamila 'Have the other vendors (Ariba, Coupa, GEP, iValua) confirmed Oman hosting compliance?' — if they have not, Zycus can position as the only vendor that took the requirement seriously and validated it early, raising the compliance bar and potentially disqualifying competitors who default to Western cloud regions.
